answer:
**Allama Iqbal's *Bang-e-Dara* (Parts 1, 2, and 3)** engages extensively with the desert (*sahra*) as a multifaceted symbol: the Arabian cradle of Islamic civilization and camel-herders who built empires; a spiritual realm to transcend in tales like Majnun and Laila; a metaphor for vastness, poverty's pride, solitude, and Hijazi culture.[^1][^2][^3]

**Cynewulf's *Andreas*** portrays the wilderness as a site of divine wonders, where God proves His kingship over creation, echoing biblical journeys.[^4]

**Mirza Ghalib's *Diwan-e-Ghalib*** uses "wilderness" metaphorically for existential dismay, love's obsession (e.g., Majnun's realm), and ruin.[^5]

Incidental mentions appear in legal texts like *Bṛhaspati Smṛti* and *Viṣṇu Smṛti* (wilderness travel rules) or *Traité de Législation* (practical hardships), but without thematic depth.[^6] No other works in the corpus engage substantially.

[^1]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 3*, Ch. 13 ["Khitab ba Jawanan-e-Islam"](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-3-cc61e8/13-khitab-ba-jawanan-e-islam/translation/#p-365462).

[^2]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 1*, Ch. 9 ["Majnoon ne sheher chhora to sehra bhi chhor de"](https://falsafa.corpus/works/allama-iqbal-bang-e-dara-part-1-55b25c/09-majnoon-ne-sheher-chhora-to-sehra-bhi-chhor-de/translation/#p-131fb4).

[^3]: Allama Iqbal, *Bang-e-Dara Part 2*, Ch. 5 ["Zamana aaya hai be-hijabi ka..."], multiple refs. to desert sands/Hijaz covenant. (See corpus search "desert" for full hits.)

[^4]: Cynewulf, *Andreas*, Ch. 1 [https://falsafa.corpus/works/cynewulf-andreas-07b573/01/translation/#p-6384bb].

[^5]: Mirza Ghalib, *Diwan-e-Ghalib*, Ch. 137 ["Meri hasti fazaaye hairat..."](https://falsafa.corpus/works/mirza-ghalib-diwan-e-ghalib-74ed4c/137-meri-hasti-fazaaye-hairat-abaad-e-tamanna-hai/translation/#p-895885).

[^6]: E.g., *Bṛhaspati Smṛti*, Ch. 1 [https://falsafa.corpus/works/unknown-brhaspati-smrti-0fd070/01/translation/#p-db1d48].
